data.name: Classic Thai Mango Sticky Rice
data.description: A beloved Thai dessert featuring perfectly steamed glutinous rice drenched in a rich, sweet, and slightly salty coconut cream sauce, paired with luscious slices of ripe mango and topped with crispy toasted mung beans.

refrigerator.notes: Glutinous rice becomes hard when chilled. Best eaten fresh.

storage.reheating: Microwave the rice briefly with a splash of water to soften if previously refrigerated.

equipment.name: Steamer
equipment.alternative: Mesh sieve set over a pot of boiling water

items.name: glutinous rice
items.notes: Also labeled as sweet rice. Do not use standard jasmine rice.

items.name: full-fat coconut milk
items.notes: Do not use light coconut milk

instructions.text: Rinse the glutinous rice under cold water until the water runs completely clear. Submerge the rice in ample water and soak at room temperature for at least 4 hours, or overnight.
tips: Washing away excess starch prevents the cooked rice from becoming gummy.

instructions.text: Drain the soaked rice. Line a steamer with damp cheesecloth, place the rice inside, and steam over rolling boiling water at 100°C/212°F for 25 to 30 minutes until the rice is tender and translucent.

doneness_cues.visual: Grains are shiny and completely translucent without chalky white centers
doneness_cues.tactile: Chewy but fully tender with no hard core

instructions.text: While the rice is steaming, combine 250ml coconut milk, palm sugar, 3g salt, and the pandan leaf in a medium saucepan. Simmer gently over medium heat until the sugar dissolves completely.
tips: Do not let the coconut milk come to a rapid boil, or it may separate and become oily.

instructions.text: Transfer the hot steamed rice to a mixing bowl. Immediately pour the hot sweet coconut sauce over the rice and gently fold to combine. Cover the bowl and let it rest for 20 minutes to absorb the liquid.
In a small bowl, mix the rice flour and water to create a smooth slurry.
In a small saucepan, heat the remaining 120ml coconut milk and 2g salt. Whisk in the rice flour slurry and simmer gently until the mixture thickens slightly.

doneness_cues.visual: Thick enough to lightly coat the back of a spoon

instructions.text: Peel the mangoes and slice the flesh diagonally into bite-sized pieces, taking care to cut around the flat inner pit.
Place a portion of the warm sticky rice on a plate next to the sliced mango. Drizzle the salty coconut topping over the rice and sprinkle with toasted mung beans.

troubleshooting.symptom: Cooked rice is crunchy or has hard white centers
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Insufficient soaking time or inadequate steam pressure
troubleshooting.prevention: Ensure rice is soaked for an absolute minimum of 4 hours and steam over a rapid boil
troubleshooting.fix: Sprinkle the rice with a little hot water, cover, and steam for an additional 10 to 15 minutes

troubleshooting.symptom: Rice is swimming in sauce and deeply mushy
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Rice or sauce cooled down too much before mixing, preventing absorption
troubleshooting.prevention: Mix the rice and sauce immediately while both are steaming hot

chef_notes: Always use glutinous rice, often sold as sweet rice. Standard short-grain or jasmine rice will not work for this dessert as they lack the necessary amylopectin structure.
Mixing the hot rice with the warm sauce immediately is critical. If the rice cools down before the sauce is introduced, the starch structure tightens and it will not absorb the coconut milk properly.
The salty topping is not optional. The deliberate saltiness balances the sweetness of the sugar and the mango, achieving the quintessential Thai flavor harmony.
Look for Nam Dok Mai or Ataulfo mangoes if available. Their floral sweetness and smooth, fiber-free texture are perfect for complementing the chewiness of the rice.
data.cultural_context: Khao Niao Mamuang is perhaps Thailand's most globally recognized dessert, traditionally peaking in popularity during the mango harvest season in April and May. The balance of sweet, salty, and rich flavors perfectly exemplifies the core philosophy of Thai cuisine, serving as a cooling contrast to a spicy savory meal.
